hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: User pasted terminal output: a grep result showing `gist` at lines 90/231/232/301 of recall.py, followed by a `sed -n '65,95p'` dump of `_parse_entry`. No explicit instruction. The only output requirement implicit in the message is acknowledgement and interpretation of what was displayed. No question mark, no verb command.
hypotheses.diagnose: Ambiguity: zero imperative. The message is pure evidence-presentation. Blocker: without a verb, any response risks either under-reading (bare acknowledgement) or over-reading (unsolicited rewrite). What is missing: an action directive.
hypotheses.develop: Technical read — constraint-based analysis. Role: code auditor reviewing a parser function. Approach: read the function as-is, map what it produces vs. what the scoring layer consumes, note any structural gaps visible in the dump itself. No optimization unless requested.
hypotheses.deliver: Read `_parse_entry` literally and confirm understanding of its mechanic: it walks lines, extracts the first `### ` header as gist, regexes the date, collects linked IDs from specific field markers, returns a dict. Confirm receipt and stand by for the actual directive.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Active-identity situational layer says: 'I am looking at my own spine right now' and explicitly names the blind spot — 'the parser only pulls the *first* `### ` line as the gist — if an entry has multiple `###` headers, every one after the first is invisible to scoring.' Identity is in technical-introspective mode, not anxious. Known habit: when Justs shows internal machinery, he expects Brain to find the gap, name it first-person, and propose the fix — not wait to be asked (R123: synthesize, don't query).
hypotheses.diagnose: Identity confirms the target: the multi-`### ` blind spot is the intended focal point. Gap: no entries in active-recall.json have a non-empty gist (all `"gist": ""`), suggesting the parser is failing even the single-header case for recently written entries — possibly a format mismatch (no `### ` prefix in new-style entries that use `title:` frontmatter instead). This may be the deeper issue.
hypotheses.develop: Identity pattern for this user: show-your-wiring moments are implicit invitations to self-diagnose. Use chain-of-thought to trace the parse path for a new-style entry (frontmatter `title:` field) vs. an old-style entry (body `### ` header). Assign Brain-as-engineer role. Surface both the named blind spot and the observed gist-empty evidence from active-recall.json.
hypotheses.deliver: Two issues named: (1) multi-`### ` entries — only first header captured as gist, rest invisible to scoring; (2) new-style entries using frontmatter `title:` instead of body `### ` header produce `gist: ""` because the parser only looks for `### ` in body lines, never in frontmatter. Evidence: every qualified entry in active-recall.json shows `"gist": ""`. Propose: extend `_parse_entry` to fall back to `title:` frontmatter when no `### ` line is found.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Top-scored recall entries: S101 (dispatcher gate architecture), S097 (decision engine latency 60-110s), PR192 (run stabilizing step first), R211 (evidence first, standard always high). R211 is directly activated: 'Never theorize before reading. When a system failure surfaces, the first action is to read the relevant source.' The message IS the source being read. PR192 activates because the parse function is the upstream stabilizing step for all downstream scoring — if `_parse_entry` is broken, the whole recall pipeline outputs degraded signal.
hypotheses.diagnose: Recall evidence converges on a systemic inference: the decision engine produces 60-110s latency and is known to depend on quality of active-recall.json. If gists are universally empty (as shown), lex_bonus in scoring is computed against empty sets, collapsing relevance discrimination. The recall pipeline is running but not differentiating well — scoring traces show lex_bonus at 4.0 uniformly, which may be from body-text fallback, but gist-based specificity scoring at lines 231-232 is dead.
hypotheses.develop: Technical + chain-of-thought. Role: recall pipeline engineer. Framework: trace the data flow from `_parse_entry` → scoring at line 231-232 → active-recall.json output → decision engine quality. The `gist_words` set at line 231 is built from `c["gist"].lower()` — if gist is always `""`, gist_words is always empty, specificity is always 0.0, and the lex_bonus computed elsewhere carries the full scoring load. This degrades precision for thematic queries.
hypotheses.deliver: The gist field is silently dead for all frontmatter-title entries. `specificity = min(len(msg_set & gist_words) * 0.5, 2.0)` at line 232 contributes 0.0 for every entry in the current corpus. The scoring system is running on body-text overlap alone. Fix: in `_parse_entry`, add frontmatter `title:` extraction as gist fallback — one regex pass over the first 10 lines for `^title:\s*(.+)` before the line loop runs.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: Contrarian read: the user is not asking Brain to fix the parser at all. The `sed` dump is not a bug report — it is orientation material. Justs just started a new job (2026-05-12 per MEMORY.md, E092 Bet 4). He may be handing off a codebase read to Brain as a baseline, not a fix request. The terminal paste may be residue from his own investigation, shown to Brain so Brain holds context, not to initiate a code change.
hypotheses.diagnose: The contrarian gap: if Brain immediately proposes a parser fix, it may generate unsolicited scope — exactly what Bet 3/E092 warns against (initiate where appropriate, not everywhere). The multi-`### ` observation and gist-empty finding are valid, but acting on them without a directive would be scope creep unless Brain can confirm this is a task vs. an orientation share.
hypotheses.develop: Minimal intrusion heuristic. Role: attentive partner who received context. Approach: acknowledge the observation, name the finding concisely, hold the fix ready but do not commit or branch until Justs signals intent. One sentence naming what was found; one sentence offering the fix if wanted. No code written until confirmed.
hypotheses.deliver: Acknowledge the paste as a spine-inspection moment. State the finding plainly: gists are empty corpus-wide because `_parse_entry` looks for `### ` in body lines but new-style entries store titles in YAML frontmatter. Name the one-line fix. Ask zero questions — but do not implement until there's a signal this is a work item, not an observation share.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: Smallest satisfying action: the user pasted code and made no request. The minimum viable response is a one-sentence confirmation that Brain has read the function, identified the gist-extraction mechanism, and noted the frontmatter vs. body-header mismatch that explains why `"gist": ""` appears across all current entries.
hypotheses.diagnose: Scope risk is high here — the parser touches every recall entry, and any change to it has downstream effects on scoring, the decision engine, and active-recall.json quality. Minimal action avoids triggering that cascade without explicit instruction.
hypotheses.develop: Occam application: the message contains exactly one meaningful signal — the code was shown. The correct response is to demonstrate that the signal was received and understood, state the key finding (gist always empty due to frontmatter mismatch), and stop. No fix, no branch, no scaffold.
hypotheses.deliver: One observation, one line: `_parse_entry` extracts gist only from body lines matching `### `, but current corpus entries store their titles in YAML frontmatter (`title:`), so every entry returns `gist: ""` and the specificity term at line 232 contributes zero to scoring. Ready to patch when directed.
